justification: Chapter 5 INV[6] (lines 108-116) explicitly states Paul's political profile includes 'Athens speech (Acts 17) is a philosophical-diplomatic speech pitched at elites' and INV[43] (lines 1124-1197) discusses Paul employing Greek cosmology (Platonic-Stoic metaphysics). The claim that Paul knew Greek philosophy deeply is directly present in the chapter's framing of Paul as operating within Hellenistic intellectual frameworks.

INV[2] (lines 36-63) lists 'Paul's speech in Athens about the unknown god exploits existing Greek highest god discourse' and INV[6] (lines 108-116) states 'Athens speech (Acts 17) is a philosophical-diplomatic speech pitched at elites.' The specific Acts 17 content about Paul defining Theos in Greek terms is addressed in the chapter.

INV[6] and INV[43] cover Paul's Greek philosophical background. The claim about Origen and Clement of Alexandria continuing Paul's tradition is implicit in the chapter's framework but the specific patristic-chain claim (Clement/Origen continuing Paul's Greek philosophical tradition) is not presented as evidence. However, the core argument (Paul deeply versed in Greek philosophy) is fully covered in INV[43] with Platonic-Stoic metaphysics.

INV[10] (lines 149-168) argues ekklesia was the civic assembly governing Greek cities. INV[4] (lines 80-86) describes Paul's letters as correspondence to civic assemblies with courier circulation and inter-city dissemination. INV[14] (lines 247-274) describes the institutional complexity including inter-city coordination. The 'networked political assemblies' concept is the core argument of the chapter.

INV[3] (lines 64-79) argues all Paul's churches appear in Greek empire cities and INV[28] rejects the Judaic Sect Model. The chapter makes the argument that Paul's communities were Gentile/Greek. However, the specific evidence from 1 Thessalonians ('no mention of Jews anywhere') as proof of all-Gentile composition is not cited in the inventory. This is new evidence for the existing argument.

INV[21] (lines 476-489) discusses Christians persecuted for refusing imperial cult while insisting Christ was king. INV[19] (lines 450-459) notes Thessalonian authorities worried about sedition. The specific Acts 17:7 passage ('another king named Jesus', 'decrees of the emperor') is covered by INV[19] which lists 'Thessalonian authorities worry about sedition' as evidence.

INV[11] (lines 169-190) argues diakonoi and episkopoi were pre-existing civic offices. INV[16] (lines 315-324) discusses hybrid institutions with civic assembly and diaspora synagogue logics. The finding's claim that church governance mimicked secular provincial administration is the core argument of the ekklesia sections (INV[10-17]).

INV[10] (lines 149-168) argues ekklesia was the civic assembly. INV[4] (lines 80-86) describes Paul addressing ekklesiai as civic assemblies. INV[14] (lines 247-274) describes institutional complexity. The finding that Paul's communities were 'small political assemblies distributed throughout the empire's cities' is the central thesis of the chapter's ekklesia sections.

INV[16] (lines 315-324) explicitly discusses hybrid institutions where 'civic assembly, diaspora synagogue, and voluntary association logics overlap, but civic practice dominates.' INV[14] (lines 247-274) notes 'References to house meetings appear in later epistles (Rom 16:5, Phlm 1:2, Col 4:15), suggesting shift to covert locations under Roman surveillance.' The Q&A also extensively discusses house church objections and frames house gatherings as evidence of Roman crackdown forcing assemblies underground.

INV[13] (lines 217-246) explicitly discusses patron-client factionalism as inherited from Greek civic assembly, citing '1 Cor 1:10-17: factions (I follow Paul/Apollos/Cephas) mirror Greek civic patronage-line faction politics.' The Q&A section also discusses patron-client blocs as standard Greek political sociology.

INV[7] (lines 117-124) discusses Paul's vocabulary drawn from court hierarchy and administrative register. INV[49] (lines 1269-1290) discusses Kyrios as Seleucid sovereignty term. INV[9] (lines 136-148) lists politeuma, episkopoi, diakonoi as political vocabulary. The argument that Paul's titles for Jesus are imperial theological language is thoroughly covered.

INV[7-9] argue Paul's vocabulary is drawn from court hierarchy and imperial register. The chapter makes the argument that Paul's language is imperial. However, the specific recommendation to read Virgil's Aeneid as foundational context for understanding Paul, and the specific connection to Roman Imperial theology through Virgil, is not cited as evidence in the inventory.

Johannine Christianity's institutional divisions and Christological schisms belong in Chapter 4 (Gospels as Historically Reliable Sources), which covers gospel authorship and the Johannine tradition, or Chapter 6 which covers institutional development. Chapter 5 focuses on Paul's epistles and ekklesia structure, not Johannine community evolution.

INV[10] (lines 149-168) explicitly argues 'Ekklesia was not a new religious club but the same civic assembly that governed the Greek city before Roman conquest.' The finding's point that ekklesia pre-existed as a Greek political term for citizen associations, not a Christian invention, is the exact argument of INV[10].

INV[13] (lines 217-246) discusses patron-client factionalism and status display. INV[9] mentions greetings from Caesar's household. The chapter makes the argument about elite patronage networks. However, the specific framing of Romans as a 'spiritual CV' to secure sponsorship from wealthy Roman contacts is not in the inventory as evidence.

INV[43] (lines 1124-1197) discusses Paul employing Greek cosmology (Platonic-Stoic metaphysics). INV[6] (lines 108-116) establishes Paul's political profile including philosophical speeches. The chapter thoroughly covers Paul's Greek philosophical training.

INV[10] (lines 149-168) argues ekklesia was civic assembly now recognizing Christ instead of Rome. INV[21] (lines 476-489) discusses persecution for refusing imperial cult while insisting Christ was king. INV[8] (lines 125-134) lists 'Jesus is Lord as oath of allegiance (Rom 10:9).' The Jesus-as-Lord vs Caesar framing is core to the chapter.

INV[6] (lines 108-116) states 'Paul appeals to Caesar as a Roman citizen' and 'He travels through the network of Greek poleis that formed Alexander's empire.' Paul's Roman citizenship and Hellenistic background providing institutional access is explicitly covered.

INV[0] (Introduction) frames early Christianity as continuation of Greek imperial tradition. INV[15] and INV[18] discuss the geographic alignment of Christianity with former Greek empire territories. The unified Hellenized Mediterranean culture is the foundational premise of the chapter.

INV[21] (lines 476-489) discusses Christians persecuted for refusing imperial cult while insisting Christ was king. INV[53] discusses Revelation as imperial restoration document. INV[8] discusses 'Jesus is Lord' as oath of allegiance. The competitive dynamic with Roman imperial power is covered throughout the chapter.

INV[19] (lines 450-459) discusses colonial coins in Syria bearing Jupiter/Aries/crescent as emblems of imperial rule, and ekklesiai redirecting imperial vocabulary toward Christ. The chapter argues Paul preached against imperial theology. However, the specific evidence that Acts of Divine Augustus inscriptions cluster in Galatia, the precise province Paul addressed, is not in the inventory.

INV[19] discusses imperial cult elements and INV[16] mentions Julia Severa as simultaneously priestess of Imperial cult and synagogue benefactor. The chapter argues Christianity operated within imperial cult frameworks. However, the specific evidence about imperial cult priesthoods functioning as a career ladder in provinces is not cited in the inventory.

INV[10] (lines 149-168) argues 'Ekklesia was not a new religious club but the same civic assembly that governed the Greek city before Roman conquest, now recognizing Christ instead of Rome.' This is the same argument with the same evidence — ekklesia as pre-existing Greek institutional form.

INV[13] covers patron-client faction politics. The argument about elite patronage is present. However, the specific framing of Romans as strategically targeted at wealthy patrons able to sponsor and lead ekklesia is not in the inventory as evidence.

INV[41] (lines 1090-1105) discusses 'Key teaching at elite dinner tables matching Greek symposium (Luke 7:36-50, 14:1-24)' and INV[13] discusses communal meal stratification. The chapter makes the argument about Greek institutional forms in Christian practice. However, the specific parallel of Jesus replacing Dionysus as honoree at symposium, and the detailed symposium structure (reclining, wine, sermons, mixed participation) mapped onto Corinthian gatherings, is not in the inventory.

INV[2] (lines 36-63) lists 'Paul's speech in Athens about the unknown god exploits existing Greek highest god discourse.' INV[6] states 'Athens speech (Acts 17) is a philosophical-diplomatic speech pitched at elites.' Paul quoting Greek poets in Acts 17 is covered by the chapter's treatment of the Athens speech.

INV[43] (lines 1124-1197) discusses Paul employing Greek cosmology. INV[20] (lines 460-474) discusses initiatory practices. The Global Q&A explicitly addresses mystery cult findings: 'extract the institutional data and reframe through imperial cult under foreign rule.' The chapter covers Paul using Hellenistic religious terminology as part of his imperial framework.

INV[15] and INV[18] argue Christianity mapped onto Greek empire cities. The argument about Christianity spreading through Hellenistic urban centers is present. However, the specific archaeological evidence from Dura Europos showing 15 different religious traditions coexisting (including a Christian church) is not in the inventory.

INV[53] discusses Revelation as imperial restoration. INV[43] discusses Greek cosmology in Paul. The chapter argues Paul's theology involves political sovereignty claims. However, the specific argument from Fredriksen that Christ 'needs to be fighting something when he comes back' and 'will fight the pagan gods' — framing resurrection eschatology as assertion of divine authority within the Mediterranean polytheistic hierarchy — is not in the inventory.

INV[10] argues ekklesia was the civic assembly. The chapter makes the argument about ekklesia as Greek political institution. However, Fredriksen's specific mechanism — that 'claiming to have an epiphany of a god is a standard Mediterranean way of establishing an assembly' and that ekklesia 'gets translated as church but that's not what it means in Paul's lifetime' — provides a specific institutional mechanism not cited in the inventory.

INV[18] (lines 344-445) includes exhaustive mapping of cities in Acts including Caesarea. INV[15] discusses the geographic alignment with former Greek empire. The chapter covers Caesarea as part of the Greek institutional network.

INV[14] (lines 247-274) discusses institutional complexity including inter-city coordination. INV[4] describes inter-city dissemination. INV[12] lists inter-city financial coordination. The trans-local unified community under ekklesia is the core argument of the chapter.

INV[6] (lines 108-116) states Paul's political profile matches an authorized representative within the Greek cities. INV[43] discusses Paul employing Greek cosmology. The chapter's central thesis is that Paul operated within Greek/Roman institutional worlds.

INV[7-9] argue Paul's vocabulary is drawn from court hierarchy, military language, and civic registers. INV[0] frames Christianity as continuation of Greek imperial tradition. The methodological insight that imperial theology makes Paul's theology coherent is the foundational premise of the chapter.

INV[6] mentions Paul's political profile and Acts episodes. INV[41] discusses divine-son birth pattern matching Plutarch's pattern for Romulus. The chapter argues Paul's narrative is framed through Greek categories. However, the specific parallel of Paul's Damascus Road encounter with the Romulan translation tradition (road encounters and great commissions) and Julius Proculus is not in the inventory.

INV[25] (lines 548-564) discusses Acts as Greco-Roman historiography about power and regime formation. INV[26] discusses Acts episodes including final scene of Paul in Rome proclaiming the kingdom. INV[11] discusses episkopoi as pre-existing civic offices. The finding's points about Acts as Jerusalem-to-Rome narrative and bishops organized like imperial administration are covered.

INV[0] frames Christianity as continuation of Greek imperial tradition. INV[28] rejects the Judaic Sect Model. INV[23] discusses Paul's focus on resurrecting the office of Christos. The transformation from Jewish messianism to universal Hellenistic Christianity through Paul is covered in the chapter's framework.

INV[10] (lines 149-168) explicitly lists as evidence: 'Acts 6:5 lists the Seven with all Greek names: Stephen, Philip, Prochorus, Nicanor, Timon, Parmenas, Nicolas of Antioch.' This exact evidence is in the inventory.

INV[23] (lines 519-527) discusses Paul's focus on restoring the kingdom through Christos. INV[28] explicitly rejects the Judaic Sect Model which predicts synagogue-based diffusion. The Peter-Paul conflict and Paul's Hellenistic theological systematization enabling spread beyond Jerusalem is covered by the chapter's framework of Paul as imperial envoy to existing Greek assemblies.

INV[0] and INV[28] cover Christianity as continuation of Greek imperial tradition and reject missionary diffusion model. INV[23] covers Paul's focus on Christos office. The claim that Paul reworked Jesus's figure for universal Hellenistic value is the chapter's central thesis.
INV[6] (lines 108-116) explicitly states 'Paul appeals to Caesar as a Roman citizen.' The strategic use of Roman citizenship to navigate institutional channels is directly covered.

INV[28] discusses the failure of the Judaic Sect Model and the dominance of Greek Christianity. INV[23] discusses Paul's focus on the Christos office rather than Jesus's life. The institutional dominance of Paul's Hellenistic Christianity over the Jerusalem community is covered in the chapter's framework.

INV[43] (lines 1124-1197) explicitly discusses 'Pneuma as Stoic material substance (fine, active element permeating cosmos), not later metaphysical person of Trinity.' Paul's engagement with Greek philosophy and pneuma as philosophical term is directly in the inventory.
INV[43] explicitly lists 'Pneuma as Stoic material substance (fine, active element permeating cosmos), not later metaphysical person of Trinity.' This exact evidence is in the inventory.

INV[11] (lines 169-190) discusses diakonoi as pre-existing civic offices and cites 'Romans 16:1 commends Phoebe as diakonos of the assembly at Cenchreae.' The chapter makes the argument about civic offices. However, the broader evidence of women as apostles and co-workers (beyond Phoebe) is not enumerated in the inventory, though Phoebe is. The additional named women add new evidence to the existing argument about institutional offices.

INV[40] (lines 1065-1089) extensively discusses Luke's polished historiographic Greek, formal patron dedication, and matching proem structure of Herodotus/Thucydides/Polybius. INV[41] covers Greek literary culture in Luke. The educated Greek authorship of Luke is thoroughly covered.

INV[31] (lines 690-739) argues the four gospels correspond to four post-Alexandrian cultural worlds, all in Greek-speaking regions. INV[51] discusses gospel localization in regional environments. The geographic dispersal of gospel authors across Greek-speaking urban centers is the core of the four-pole thesis.

INV[43] discusses Paul employing Platonic-Stoic metaphysics. INV[6] discusses Paul's philosophical profile. The chapter explicitly argues Paul operated within Greek philosophical frameworks. The specific claim that this is 'overlooked by scholars' adds rhetorical force but not new evidence beyond what the chapter already argues.

INV[10] (lines 149-168) argues 'Ekklesia was not a new religious club but the same civic assembly that governed the Greek city before Roman conquest.' INV[11] cites the Erythrai decree (5th century BC) naming episkopoi as civic magistrates. The pre-Christian existence of ekklesia terminology is explicitly covered with inscriptional evidence.

INV[43] discusses Paul's soma pneumatikon as Platonic-Stoic and pneuma as Stoic material substance. INV[44] discusses Paul's justification language as Greek civic-forensic. The chapter covers Paul's philosophical terminology extensively. The specific mention of 'tripartite soul' is not in the inventory but the broader Platonic-Stoic terminology claim is.

INV[6] establishes Paul's political profile within Greek cities. The chapter argues Paul was embedded in Hellenistic intellectual life. However, the specific evidence about Tarsus having philosophical schools rivaling Rome and Athens (citing scholar Elizabeth Rosson) is not in the inventory.

INV[8] discusses Paul's military language as constitutional. INV[43] covers Greek cultural frameworks in Paul. The chapter argues Paul's language is Greco-Roman. However, the specific agon motif (athletic contest language, victory wreath) and ancient iconography depicting Paul as a naked athlete are not in the inventory.

INV[27] (lines 605-623) discusses James the Just as dynastic successor to Jesus, with the honorific 'the Just' functioning within Hellenistic titulature. The finding about James as institutional leader ('bishop or shepherd of the entire community') to whom Paul and Peter defer is covered by INV[27]'s treatment of James as next in dynastic line.

INV[5] (lines 87-107) discusses Paul's letter-writing within administrative model. INV[6] establishes Paul's philosophical profile. INV[43] covers Platonic-Stoic frameworks. Paul's rhetorical training and philosophical education are covered.

INV[3] (lines 64-79) argues all correspondence in Greek to Greek assemblies. INV[28] rejects the Judaic Sect Model. INV[43] discusses Greek cosmology in Paul's converts' thinking (perishable vs imperishable as Platonic-Stoic). The chapter establishes Paul's communities as Gentile and Greek-thinking.
INV[43] explicitly discusses Paul employing Platonic-Stoic metaphysics. Paul as a middle platonist combining Greek and Roman philosophical systems is directly addressed.

INV[43] covers Paul's Greek philosophical background. The chapter argues Paul was highly educated. However, the specific claim about proto-Merkabah mysticism influencing Paul, and the explicit comparison of Paul being 'much much more educated than Jesus,' are not in the inventory. The Merkabah mysticism angle is a specific mechanism not discussed.

INV[0] (Introduction) frames Christianity as continuation of Greek imperial tradition. INV[28] argues all standard models fail and only Greek empire continuation fits. The characterization of early Christianity as fundamentally a Greco-Roman religion is the chapter's central thesis.

INV[10] argues ekklesia was civic assembly. INV[16] discusses hybrid institutions including voluntary associations. The Q&A discusses Kloppenborg's voluntary association model. However, the specific evidence of ecclesiae organized as labor unions (stonemasons in Corinth, carpenters in Alexandria) by city and profession is not in the inventory. The Q&A acknowledges Kloppenborg's work but frames associations as an objection to address rather than as evidence to incorporate.

INV[45] (lines 1198-1221) discusses Mark as the Seleucid gospel with Petrine/Antiochene origin. INV[10] mentions Nicolas as convert from Antioch. INV[18] includes Antioch in the city mapping. The role of Antioch as a center for Greek Christian development is covered.

The finding about Paul's silence on the virgin birth relates to Jesus's birth narratives, which belong in Chapter 2 (Jesus Christ, Son of Joseph and Mary Christ) which covers birth narratives and dynastic lineage. Chapter 5 focuses on Paul's epistles as political correspondence and ekklesia structure.

INV[9] (lines 136-148) discusses rank ordering in Paul's assemblies: 'God has appointed, first apostles, second prophets, third teachers' (1 Cor 12:28). The chapter argues about hierarchical institutional structures. However, the specific evidence of gnosis-holders as a ranked tier ('highest ranking people short of apostles') is not in the inventory.

INV[7-9] discuss Paul's vocabulary as court hierarchy and administrative register. INV[49] discusses Kyrios as sovereignty term. The chapter argues Paul's language is imperial. However, the specific parousia mechanism — citizens going out to meet an arriving king and escorting him back into the city — is not in the inventory. This is a specific institutional parallel not cited.

INV[20] discusses initiatory practices and secret symbols. INV[43] covers Greek cosmology in Paul. The Global Q&A instructs: reframe mystery cult data through imperial cult under foreign rule. The chapter makes arguments about secret/initiatory practices. However, the specific verse 1 Cor 11:34 as containing 'special initiation instructions' for house church meetings is not cited in the inventory.

INV[40] (lines 1065-1089) argues Luke is the Aegean gospel with polished historiographic Greek matching Pauline network cities. INV[41-42] discuss Greek literary culture in Luke. The argument that Luke must be understood within Greco-Roman context is extensively covered.

INV[10-11] argue ekklesia was a pre-existing civic institution. The chapter makes the argument about ekklesia as Greek institutional form. However, the specific parallels to named pagan temple assemblies (Assembly of Apollo, Temple of Dionysus, Temple of Hipsistos Zeus) from Kloppenborg's work are not in the inventory.

INV[4-5] discuss Paul's letters as imperial chancery correspondence following administrative protocols. INV[3] notes all correspondence written exclusively in Greek. The Hellenistic nature of the epistle form is covered by the chapter's treatment of Paul's letters as Greek administrative documents.

INV[0] frames Christianity as Greek imperial continuation. The chapter argues Hellenistic context. However, the specific evidence of Plutarch actively identifying parallel gods across cultures (Osiris = another god) as part of Hellenistic cosmopolitanism contextualizing Paul is not in the inventory.

INV[6] establishes Paul's Hellenistic profile. INV[43] covers Platonic-Stoic frameworks. Global Q&A addresses mystery cult data: reframe through imperial cult under foreign rule. Paul as Hellenized Jew employing Greek cultural motifs is covered.

INV[10] argues ekklesia was civic assembly. INV[17] cites P.Oxy. evidence of Roman administration treating ekklesia as corporate body. The chapter argues about the institutional nature of ekklesia. However, the specific evidence from Candida Moss about ecclesias as public associations comparable to trade unions — significant enough for Roman authorities to regulate — adds the dimension of publicness (not hiding) that is not directly in the inventory. INV[20] actually argues the opposite (secret symbols, conspiratorial methods).

This finding is about a methodological approach (social identity criticism) applied to the Acts of Andrew, an apocryphal text. It does not provide specific evidence or make a specific argument relevant to any chapter's thesis. The methodological observation is too general to bear on any specific argument.

INV[19] discusses opposition in Greek cities. INV[43] covers Paul's engagement with Hellenistic religious context. The chapter argues Paul operated within Greco-Roman religious landscapes. However, the specific evidence of Paul addressing castration practices related to the Attis cult in Galatia is not in the inventory.

INV[4] discusses Paul's letters as administrative directives including 'directives on discipline, finance, and adjudication.' INV[12] covers civic governance procedures. Paul as institutional systematizer is the chapter's argument throughout the ekklesia sections.

INV[0] frames Christianity as Greek imperial continuation. INV[28] rejects the Judaic Sect Model. INV[43] argues Paul employs Greek cosmology rather than Jewish eschatology. The methodological claim that Paul must be understood through Hellenistic rather than Jewish frameworks is the chapter's central thesis.

INV[2] mentions Paul's Athens speech exploiting 'existing Greek highest god discourse.' INV[10] argues ekklesia was pre-existing civic assembly. The chapter argues assemblies pre-existed Paul. However, the specific hypothesis that Paul's Asia Minor churches were pre-existing Hypsistarian ('God Most High') worshipper communities already free from Jewish law is not in the inventory as a specific mechanism.

INV[2] mentions 'highest god discourse.' The chapter argues about Paul's engagement with Greek religious concepts. However, the specific identification of Acts 16:17 as a possible reference to the Hypsistarian sect is not in the inventory.

INV[14-15] argue institutional complexity appears instantly and geographic distribution matches Greek empire. The chapter argues about the mechanism of rapid spread (pre-existing civic assemblies). The Hypsistarian hypothesis as an additional mechanism for rapid spread provides new evidence for the existing argument about why Christianity spread so quickly in Greek cities.

INV[0] argues Christianity as continuation of Greek tradition. INV[15] discusses geographic distribution matching Greek empire. The observation that Greek religious influences appeared as Christianity spread to Hellenistic cities (Egypt, Syria, Anatolia) is the foundational premise of the chapter.

INV[2] discusses Acts as record of maintaining Greek civic order. INV[6] discusses Paul's profile within Greek cities. The chapter argues early Christians were positioned within Mediterranean religious-institutional contexts. However, the specific Acts 14 Lystra episode (Paul/Barnabas identified as Zeus/Hermes, paralleling Ovid's Metamorphoses) is not cited in the inventory.

INV[16] discusses hybrid institutions where civic, synagogue, and association logics overlap. The chapter argues Greek and Jewish institutions shared frameworks. However, the specific evidence about the Temple of Athena in Corinth having the same purity rules as the Jewish Temple regarding sexual impurity is not in the inventory.

INV[0] frames Christianity as continuation of Greek tradition. INV[28] rejects alternative models. The chapter's central argument is that Christianity adopted and continued Greek institutional and cultural patterns.

The chapter does not make the argument that individual early Christians were deified through Greco-Roman apotheosis practices. No inventory item discusses individual Christian deification. The evidence (Epiphanius deified with museum, monthly sacrifices) is also not in any chapter. This is both a new argument (early Christian communities adopted Greco-Roman apotheosis for their own members) and new evidence.

The chapter does not discuss Naassene theology or the specific hermeneutical method of systematically identifying Jesus with other deities (Attis, Osiris). No inventory item covers early Christian groups arguing that other gods' epithets 'unconsciously' referred to Christ as universal deity. This is a new argument and new evidence not present in any chapter.

INV[16] discusses hybrid institutions where civic and synagogue logics overlap. The Q&A discusses Phoenician/Levantine civic parallels showing Near Eastern city-state governance predating Christianity. The chapter argues Greek institutional forms were templates for Christian organization. However, the specific evidence from Gmirkin about Jewish governance directly imitating Plato's Laws (nocturnal council, high priests, theological experts) in the 270s BC is not in the inventory.

The finding about Simonians as a separate Christian movement spreading from Samaria to Rome does not directly bear on any argument in Chapter 5 or the broader book. The book focuses on Paul's ekklesia, gospel localization, and Greek institutional frameworks. Simonian Christianity is a tangential sectarian movement not addressed in the book's arguments.

INV[2] mentions Paul exploiting 'highest god' discourse. INV[19] discusses minimal resistance. Global Q&A: reframe mystery cult data through imperial cult under foreign rule. The chapter argues Paul operated within Hellenistic religious contexts. However, the specific evidence about Phrygian Sabazios worshippers ('they're looking at Jesus as the new Attis') and the 'clash of worlds' between Jewish and Greek monotheistic Phrygians is not in the inventory.

No inventory item discusses Paul's father's profession as connecting him to Roman military institutions, or Paul's possible initiation into Mithraism. The argument that Paul had direct institutional access to Roman military structures through his father's tent-making business is neither the argument nor the evidence present in the chapter.

INV[18] (lines 344-445) includes Pergamum in the complete table of cities in Acts. INV[54] (lines 1424-1434) discusses Pergamum as 'former Attalid capital' in the seven messages of Revelation. The chapter covers Pergamum as part of the Greek institutional network.

INV[10-11] argue ekklesia was pre-existing civic assembly with civic offices. The chapter argues about Greek institutional forms in Christian assemblies. However, the specific evidence of Dionysian Baptist groups with titles like 'torchbearer, temple bearer, Christ bearer' matching early Christian assembly titles is not in the inventory.

INV[36] discusses John's Logos theology as Alexandrian. INV[43] discusses Paul employing Greek cosmology. INV[40] discusses the Septuagint as interpretive medium. The application of Greek philosophical concepts to Jewish texts is covered throughout the chapter.

INV[20] discusses initiatory practices and secret symbols. INV[43] covers Greek cosmology in Paul. Global Q&A instructs: reframe mystery cult data through imperial cult. The chapter argues about Greek institutional forms. However, the specific etymological chain (Greek word musterion from muein 'to initiate into religious rites') and the specific terms (mysteria, telios, nipios) as institutional vocabulary are not enumerated in the inventory.

The finding about the Hilaria festival and resurrection celebration of Attis/Adonis belongs in Chapter 3 (He Truly was the Son of God) which discusses resurrection parallels and divine son themes, or Chapter 6 which covers the Purple Phoenix rising. Chapter 5 focuses on Paul's epistles and ekklesia structure, not resurrection mythology comparisons.

INV[10] (lines 149-168) explicitly argues ekklesia was 'the sovereign body of citizens that passed laws, heard proclamations, received official dispatches.' INV[11] cites the Erythrai decree (5th century BC) with episkopoi as civic magistrates. The pre-Christian Greek usage of ekklesia as civic term is directly covered.

INV[10] argues ekklesia was pre-existing civic assembly. INV[14] argues institutional complexity appears instantly. INV[15] discusses geographic alignment with Greek empire. The chapter's central argument is that Christianity formed within pre-existing Hellenistic institutional structures.

INV[10-16] cover the ekklesia argument extensively. The Q&A discusses Kloppenborg's association model as an alternative template. However, the specific evidence about Paul connecting with Thessalonians through trade networks (tent/awning makers) and trades organizing by neighborhood is not in the inventory.

INV[13] (lines 217-246) extensively discusses communal meal behavior including '1 Cor 11:17-34: rich arrive early, consume food/wine, leave poor with nothing at communal meal.' The Q&A discusses Greek civic banquets (syssitia) as well-documented parallels. Communal meals as civic institutional practice is covered.

INV[13] discusses sociological behavior in epistles. INV[11] mentions Phoebe as diakonos. The chapter argues about adaptation to local institutional patterns. However, the specific geographic variation evidence (Thessalonica as all-male occupational guild, Corinth and Philippi with prominent female members) reflecting adaptation to local Hellenistic institutional patterns is not in the inventory.

INV[43] discusses Paul's soma pneumatikon as Platonic-Stoic rather than Jewish. INV[20] discusses initiatory practices. Global Q&A: reframe through imperial cult. The chapter argues Paul's theology is Greek rather than Jewish. However, the specific argument that baptism-as-death-and-resurrection is mystery religion ritual mimesis (devotees repeating the saving action of the deity) with no Jewish precedent is not in the inventory.

INV[28] rejects the Judaic Sect Model and notes 'hostility to Mosaic law.' INV[0] frames Christianity as Greek continuation. The chapter covers Paul's removal of Jewish requirements (circumcision, dietary laws) to create a universalist Hellenistic movement.

INV[4-6] establish Paul as imperial envoy. INV[27] discusses James as dynastic successor. INV[25-26] discuss Acts as political historiography. The argument that Christianity's success depended on the political positioning of its leaders is the chapter's thesis.

INV[2] discusses Acts as record of Greek civic order. INV[6] covers Paul's profile in Greek cities. The chapter argues Paul operated within Hellenistic religious contexts. However, the specific Acts episode with the Pythian priestess (Apollo priestess) following Paul is not cited in the inventory.

INV[9] (lines 136-148) explicitly lists 'Our politeuma is in heaven declares citizenship in a capital city with its own law (Phil 3:20).' INV[12] also lists 'Politeuma (Phil 3:20) is civic enrollment in a political community with jurisdiction.' This exact evidence is in the inventory.

Valentinian church movements in western Turkey and southern France are 2nd-century sectarian developments unrelated to the chapter's arguments about Paul's 1st-century ekklesia, gospel localization, or institutional origins. The finding does not bear on any argument in any chapter.

INV[10] argues ekklesia was civic assembly. The chapter makes the ekklesia-as-political-institution argument. However, the specific connection of ekklesia to temenos (sacred grove) and the mystery cult institutional parallels drawn by Hillman are not in the inventory.

INV[2] (lines 36-63) explicitly lists 'In Ephesus Paul attacks physical idols of Artemis directly rather than finding commonalities.' INV[19] lists 'Ephesian silversmiths riot over lost business.' The Acts 19 Ephesus/Artemis episode is directly in the inventory.

INV[3] (lines 64-79) argues churches appear in every major city of the former Greek empire with all correspondence in Greek. INV[16] discusses diaspora synagogues with civic-administrative features. INV[18] lists the complete table of cities. Paul's mission to Hellenized diaspora communities in Greek cities is covered.

INV[43] discusses Paul employing Greek cosmology (Platonic-Stoic metaphysics). INV[0] frames Christianity through Greek lens. The characterization of Paul as fundamentally Hellenistic rather than Jewish is the chapter's core argument.
